properties.field_incident_overview:
Evacuations: Curry County Sheriff John Ward has removed all evacation levels for the Moon Complex. 

The Emergency Area and Trail closure for the Moon Complex that went into effect November 14th of 2025, has been rescinded. Effective, February 24th, 2026, this closure was replaced with Emergency Trail Closure 06-10-28-26-04, which re-opens many of the camping areas along the lower Rogue River Corridor. While much of the fire-impacted area has been reopened, recreationists will continue to see effects from the fire and ongoing repair work in the area. 

For closure information, visit: https://www.fs.usda.gov/r06/rogue-siskiyou/alerts/rogue-river-national-recreation-trail-emergency-closure 

The remaining Rogue River Trail Closure begins at the western trailhead of the Rogue River Trail (Trail #1160) and follows the trail to the east for approximately 8 miles to Jackson Creek. The trail from Jackson Creek to Clay Hill Creek will be opened by May 1, 2026. The Forest will be assessing the segment from Clay Hill Creek to Foster Bar in the spring of 2026. 

On September 3, a lightning event on the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest caused a number of fires in the vicinity of the Rogue River about 11 miles northeast of Agness, Oregon. Six of these made up the initial Moon Complex: the Backbone, Brushy, Pinnacle, Tate, Paradise, and Stair fires. 

The Moon Complex is now in 100% containment status. 

Fire Restrictions: Tuesday, October 16, 2025, The Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest decreased the Fire Danger Rating to LOW across all five Ranger Districts and removed Public Use Restrictions forest-wide as well as on the Wild & Scenic section of the Rogue River. To learn more, visit the forest website: https://www.fs.usda.gov/alerts/rogue-siskiyou/alerts-notices

Fire Repair: Repair of fire line and roadways is the primary focus of resources engaged on the Moon Complex
properties.title: Moon Complex

properties.field_incident_overview:
On September 6, 2025, USFS fire managers were able to assess a new fire start caused by lightning, named the Tunnel Creek Fire, located in a remote area of the Buckhorn Wilderness in Olympic National Forest.

The fire is roughly 10 miles west of Quilcene, Washington and 1.5 miles west of the Tunnel Creek Trailhead accessed from FS Road 2740.

Updates related to the Tunnel Creek Fire will be shared on the Bear Gulch Fire Inciweb page as they are being managed by the same team.
properties.title: Tunnel Creek Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: On Wednesday, November 5, 2025, a private landowner detected smoke south of Circleville, WV in Pendleton County. Pendleton County emergency personnel immediately contacted the USDA Forest Service who responded. This wildfire is being managed under unified command with the State of West Virginia and the USDA Forest Service, Monongahela National Forest.  Initial acreage was estimated at 50 acres, with active, wind driven fire. The fire is burning in an oak-hickory forest type, with very steep, mountainous terrain making ground access difficult. Safety of firefighters and the public are the top priorities.
properties.title: Woodside Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
WEBSITE ANNOUCEMENT: The Southern Area Incident Management Gray Team is no longer in command of the fire, and this website is no longer being monitored. Please see the official Big Cypress National Preserve website at www.nps.gov/BICY for updates on the fire including area closures. If you have additional questions, please contact the Big Cypress National Preserve duty officer at 561-923-5228.

The National Fire is burning within the Big Cypress National Preserve, approximately 25 miles east of Naples, Florida. It was discovered late on Sunday, February 22. The cause is unknown and under investigation. Initial attack efforts were unsuccessful due to rapid spread in difficult terrain. Local U.S. Wildland Fire Service fire managers transferred command of the fire to the Southern Area Gray Incident Management Team, led by Incident Commander Fulton Jeansonne, at 6:00 am on Thursday, February 26, 2026. On Monday, March 9th, 2026 command of the fire will be transferred back to the local unit. 

The fire is being managed under a full suppression strategy utilizing both direct and indirect tactics, including both ground firefighters and aerial resources. 

Area weather and environmental conditions are contributed to the difficulty of suppressing the National Fire. Factors include lack of rainfall, ongoing Stage 3 drought, recent frost kill of some vegetation, La Nina conditions, and multiple cold fronts with north winds.

The National Fire is burning in sawgrass prairies, pinelands, hardwood hammocks and cypress swamps.

SPECIAL NOTE: The National Fire location as shown on the main INCIWeb map contains an error. See the February 28 information map in the "maps" section for the current map. The fire is entirely east of SR-29. There is no fire west of SR-29.
properties.title: National

properties.field_incident_overview: The Cypress Creek Wilderness Fire in the Upland Island Wilderness Area started on Tuesday, February 24, 2026. The fire is located approximately 5.9 miles from Zavalla, TX in the Angelina National Forest.
properties.title: Cypress Creek Wilderness Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Road 203 Fire was detected at 4:00 pm Thursday, March 12, 2026 on the Bessey Ranger District of the Nebraska National Forest. Ground crews initially responded and received assistance from air resources conducting retardant and water drops. Rocky Mountain Complex IMT 3 assumed command of the fire on Saturday, March 14, 2026.

The Road 203 Fire is burning in heavy dead and down timber and grass. The terrain is steep and remote, making it challenging for firefighters to get in or out of the incident. A Nebraska National Forest closure order is in effect.
properties.title: Road 203 Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Qury Fire started on March 12, 2026 in Custer, SD. The Qury and Deer Fires were initially individual fires but have combined to one fire. There are evacuation orders in place and roads are closed. For up to date information about evacuations, where to take animals, and road closures, refer to Custer County Emergency Management https://www.facebook.com/CusterCountySDEM/
properties.title: Qury Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
During the late afternoon of Thursday, March 12, 2026, a wildfire started in Morrill County, Nebraska. The fire was rapidly pushed to the east/southeast by extremely high winds. Despite the heroic efforts of firefighters and community members, the fire extended into Garden, Arthur and Keith Counties. In less than 12 hours, the fire traveled approximately 70 miles. Winds shifted to the northeast early Friday morning, March 13 and the fire is close to the western edge of Grant County. Air tankers arrived on scene early Friday morning. Local law enforcement agencies issued evacuation orders in their jurisdictions and evacuation shelters were established. Agricultural irrigation systems were activated in some areas to try to slow the fire’s spread and protect resources and structures. 

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) assumed management of the Morrill and Cottonwood Fires yesterday evening. We want to acknowledge the dedication and professionalism of the local resources who have been working these challenging fires. The team will coordinate closely with local resources. When wildfires increase in complexity and require more resources, CIMTs are ordered to help manage and support the incident. The Rocky Mountain CIMT1 is comprised of local, state and federal personnel largely from Colorado, Nebraska, South Dakota and Wyoming. 

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. The Governor’s executive order prohibiting burning will remain in effect until 11:59 p.m. on March 27.  

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

Please visit this link for additional information sources.
properties.title: Morrill Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
A large wildfire started in the southeast corner of Lincoln County during late afternoon on Thursday, March 12 and entered Dawson County later that night. Winds from the west were very high and quickly spread the fire.  Numerous evacuations took place as the fire rapidly spread to the south/southeast. Road closures occurred due to the impact of fire and smoke. Firefighters and community members heroically fought the fire since it started.  

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) assumed management of the Morrill and Cottonwood Fires Saturday evening. We want to acknowledge the dedication and professionalism of the local resources who have been working these challenging fires. The Team will coordinate closely with local resources. When wildfires increase in complexity and require more resources, CIMTs are ordered to help manage and support the incident. The Rocky Mountain CIMT1 is comprised of local, state and federal personnel largely from Colorado, Nebraska, South Dakota and Wyoming. 

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. The Governor’s executive order prohibiting burning will remain in effect until 11:59 p.m. on March 27.  

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

Please visit this link for additional information sources.
properties.title: Cottonwood Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: This fire was reported on March 18, 2026 at 1:21pm 2 miles southeast of Rockerville, South Dakota.
properties.title: Coyote Flats Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Bright Lights Fire started late in the afternoon on March 18, 2026 and is located approximately 6 miles southeast of Claude, Texas. Texas A&M Forest Service was requested to provide assistance in the form of additional firefighters, aircraft and heavy equipment. Local fire departments on the scene include Armstrong, Panhandle, Groom, Pantex and Randall. Texas A&M Forest Service and local fire departments are in joint command of the fire. 

Forward progression of the fire has been stopped as of 8:00 PM on 3/16/26. The fire is currently being held with retardant line. Heavy equipment remains working to construct line where they are able. 

Firefighters are continuing to address any areas of concern within containment lines. Patrol and mop-up will continue today, March 22, 2026.

As of the afternoon of Sunday, March 22, the fire is 100% contained. This is the last planned update for this fire.
properties.title: Bright Lights Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
No further updates will be provided - Wildfire at 100 % containment

Firefighters are responding to a 10-acre wildfire on the Cuba Ranger District, south of the Nacimiento Mine and south of NM State Road 126. Smoke may be visible from Cuba and surrounding communities.

Multiple resources are on scene and firefighters are actively engaged in a direct extinguishment strategy.
properties.title: Nacimiento Wildfire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Spring Pine Fire started the evening of March 22, 2026 and is located approximately 3 miles east of Bastrop, Texas. The fire was initially responded to by local fire departments. Texas A&M Forest Service was requested to provide assistance in the form of additional firefighters and heavy equipment. Texas A&M Forest Service and local fire departments are in joint command of the fire. 

Active fire behavior with group tree torching was observed. Firefighters worked to develop a plan for a firing operation using existing fire breaks. These fire breaks were improved in preparation for a safe firing operation. The fire bumped to Highway 21 where locals worked to hold the fire there as dozers began direct line construction. Several structures on the north side of the fire were voluntarily evacuated. 

Resources worked through Tuesday March 24th patrolling and mopping up hot spots. They determined the fire to be 100% contained. 

As of the evening of March 24, 2026,the fire is 100% contained. This will be the final update on Spring Pine Fire.
properties.title: Spring Pine Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Hutchinson Fire started just before noon on March 24, 2026 and is located approximately 2.5 miles northeast of Sanford, TX. The fire was initially responded to by local fire departments. Texas A&M Forest Service was requested to provide assistance in the form of additional firefighters, aircraft and heavy equipment. 

Fixed wing and rotor aviation resources are currently over the fire. On the ground resources are currently engaged on the left flank of the fire. 

 Evacuations were in effected but are CURRENTLY LIFTED for the following area: residents in the area of FM 687 between the Dam and Charles Hard Lane (Rockin' 22 area). A closure for FM 687 was previously in effect, but it is currently open.
properties.title: Hutchinson Fire
